One more suggestion for update:

Provide EVM support for Byzantium hard fork:
specifically:
4 new EVM opcodes:
-- 0xFD REVERT : introduced in 0.4.10 with "require()" vs. previous "throw" or "assert". Revert opcode does revert all the states AND return remaining gas, compared to "throw" who consumes all the gas in an fictitious loop. 
-- 0x3D RETURNDATASIZE : similar to CALLDATASIZE, just extends to return data as well. This enables nice error messages.
-- 0x3E RETURNDATACOPY : similar to CALLDATACOPY, extends to return data. 
-- 0xFA STATICCALL : explicit opcode to distinguish read-only operation/query and write operations, to mitigate possible re-entrancy bug

@tjlang<https://github.com/tjlang> My plan with this is to keep all the existing attributes and methods of the current Radar object intact and in the same format. A few methods will be added to accommodate the new features (get_sweep, next, ...).

The one change that I can think of that could possibly break existing code is that the base class of the Radar object will change from the current object to one of the Abstract Base Classes (ABC) from the collections module<https://docs.python.org/2/library/collections.html#collections-abstract-base-classes>. Radar objects would still be instances of pyart.core.Radar and object but also be instances of some of the ABCs. If you are subclassing the Radar object you would either need to implement the abstract methods (iter, getitem, ...) or the existing methods will be used.

     entity = get_entity_somehow()

I think I would normalize the changelog into imperative mode.  E.g.:

### General
- Update required version of `protobuf` to `3.0.0-alpha-1`.
- Add `from_service_account_json`, `from_service_account_p12` and `from_environment` factories to `Connection` base class, allowing creation of a connection without first creating a set of credentials.

### Datastore
-   Fix serious bug where the transaction ID was not passed along to transactions, effectively making a transaction act as a batch.
-   Add appropriate scope to un-scoped credentials passed to connection constructor.
-   Enable `connection` as an optional argument to all methods and functions which use a connection.
-   Require `exclude_from_indexes` to be a tuple or list in `Entity` constructor.
-   Require `projection`, `order` and `group_by` to be a tuple or list in `Query` constructor.
-   Re-purpose `datastore.get` to take a single key;  move previous functionality to `datastore.get_multi`. Similar
    changes were made for

    ```
    datastore.put --> datastore.put_multi
    ```

    and

    ```
    datastore.delete --> datastore.delete_multi
    ```

### Storage
-   Add support for "futures", to allow GET/PATCH to work correctly in a batch.
-   Remove default chunk size in upload/download.
-   Add lazily-loaded default connection and project.
-   Remove connection property from `Blob`/`ACL`/`Bucket` classes.
-   Enable `connection` as an optional argument to all methods and functions which use a connection.
-   Add appropriate scope to un-scoped credentials passed to connection constructor.
-   Update public blob URL to use `storage.googleapis.com`.
-   Remove `Bucket.__iter__` and `Bucket.__contains__`.
-   Limit `Bucket.make_public(recursive=True)` to abort if more than 256 objects are in the bucket. This is similar to what is done in `Bucket.delete(force=True)`.

### Pub/Sub
-   Add an option to auto-add a timestamp to published, messages, allowing them to be sorted / logged.
-   Add appropriate scope to un-scoped credentials passed to connection constructor.
-   Enable `connection` as an optional argument to all methods and functions which use a connection.
-   Fix a bug in `Subscription.pull` for cases when `receivedMessages` is not returned.

What download resume methods does gcloud-python support - in other words, how do you recover from transient service or network issues?  If support is based on downloading entire chunks successfully, then chunk size is implicitly a tradeoff between performance vs. reliability.

But ideally you wouldn't have a chunk size at all - you would request the entire object in a single request, and in the presence of a transient network or service error, pick up where you left off based on how many bytes you had successfully downloaded.

import os
 import io
def transcribe_file(speech_file):
    """Transcribe the given audio file."""
    from google.cloud import speech
    from google.cloud.speech import enums
    from google.cloud.speech import types
    client = speech.SpeechClient()

    with io.open(speech_file, 'rb') as audio_file:
        content = audio_file.read()

    audio = types.RecognitionAudio(content=content)
    config = types.RecognitionConfig(
        encoding='FLAC',
        sample_rate_hertz=44100,
        language_code='en-US')

    response = client.recognize(config, audio)
    alternatives = response.results[0].alternatives

    for alternative in alternatives:
        print('Transcript: {}'.format(alternative.transcript))

Previously, BuildLocalPythonDistributions generated local dists and put them
in products. Downstream tasks then synthesized PythonRequirementLibrary
targets to point to the local dists, if they needed to resolve them. This was clunky,
and required special knowledge of local dist issues to reside in multiple downstream
tasks.

This change has BuildLocalPythonDistributions itself inject the synthetic
PythonRequirementLibrary targets - one per dist - and stitches them into the
build graph. Downstream tasks no longer need to know or care about local dists
(apart from declaring them as required products), they just see PythonRequirementLibrary
targets, whether synthetic or organic, and handle them uniformly.

Note that previously PythonBinaryCreate had to do some footwork to ensure that only the
dists depended on by a specific PythonBinary were used when creating that binary.
This now happens naturally, since we generate one requirement library per dist,
and the dependencies are wired up in the right way.

This is dangerous as new releases of mxnet are not always backwards compatible. This is why we have the ==.

Actually, Model Server handles this for you with the gen-api feature.
Gen-api can generate the client code for you in most high level languages, including Python.

To use it, follow these steps:
1. Generate client code for python using mxnet-model-server as detailed [here](https://github.com/awslabs/mxnet-model-server/blob/master/docs/code_gen.md)
1. See that a `/build` directory was created where you executed the model server command - this dir contains the auto generated client code
1. cd into the /build dir
1. Install the package, e.g. `python setup.py install --user`
1. Start the python shell, and then:
```python
import swagger_client.apis as api
client = api.DefaultApi()
client.ping()
# You should see {'health': 'healthy!'}, or otherwise your server is probably not running...
client.squeezenet_predict("<file path>") # change the method name per the model name you are using
# You should see the prediction result
```

If you are interested to see the auto-gen python code, and how it is posting the file, you can always look into the generated code.

HTH!

Indeed it seems to be a bug, which disregards the dependencies between these resources. I am using SAM CLI 0.4.0 and AWS CLI 1.11.131 and had the same problem.
In my case the final template generate by Cloudformation produced the stage name as a concatenation of my API logical name and word `Stage`: `<myApiLogicalName>Stage`. 

Fixed the problem by adding a `DependsOn` on `AWS::ApiGateway::BasePathMapping` as seen below:

```
  # ########################################################################
  # Mobile API
  apiMobile:
    Type: 'AWS::Serverless::Api'
    Properties:
      Name: mobile-api
      StageName: !FindInMap [ApiStage, !Ref Environment, stage]
      DefinitionBody:
        'Fn::Transform':
          Name: 'AWS::Include'
          Parameters:
            Location: 's3://<redacted>/swagger.yaml'
      EndpointConfiguration: REGIONAL

  # ###################################################################
  # Base Path Mapping
  apiBasePathMapping:
    Type: 'AWS::ApiGateway::BasePathMapping'
    Properties:
      BasePath: v1.0
      Stage: !FindInMap [ApiStage, !Ref Environment, stage]
      DomainName:
        'Fn::Sub':
          - 'mobile-api${Suffix}.<redacted>'
          - Suffix: !FindInMap
            - EnvironmentShort
            - !Ref Environment
            - suffix
      RestApiId: !Ref apiMobile
    DependsOn:
      - apiMobileStage  <------ Added explicit dependency
```

> It looks good in itself, but I'm wondering if we shouldn't separate the schema from the Message class to make sure devs don't do weird things by subclassing the validate() method. I've tried this split here[1], what do you think?
>
> I've also separated the methods that extract (human-readable) information, because those methods don't have to change with each schema revision, so I though we could point to the message "extractor" or "parser" class. (I don't particularly like the term "extractor").

I think we should make an effort to stop developers from accidentally shooting themselves in the foot, but I don't think we should (or even can) stop developers from that kind of failing. We should document that ``validate`` should not be overridden in a sub-class if the developer wants any benefit from the schema they just wrote, of course, but a truly determined individual can overcome whatever obstacles we put in their way if they want to make their lives difficult.

I do think the separation of "parser" from schema is interesting, but I don't think we need to enforce that pattern. A user could write their own ``MessageExtractor`` class and use multiple inheritance with it and the ``Message`` class rather than having it as attributes. Or, if they don't like that, they can write the one class. I'm generally nervous about adding lots of tiny classes that need to be combined to make a useful object. Might be worth documenting that approach, though?

> But there are still several special cases that crop up where we can hide the details of dataset management.

I agree that it would be nice to avoid requiring devs to specify the dataset as an argument, when creating models for which the dataset can be inferred. I was thinking about having the `clean` and `save` methods infer the dataset if possible, and set it themselves, rather than throwing an error if it's missing. Because the logic for inferring the dataset varies by model, I'm thinking to add a method called `infer_dataset`, which can get overridden to return the appropriately inferred dataset for the current model. I'll try that out.

> We clearly identified what it means to be an "admin for a facility" (and coach->classroom, learner->learner group). It makes a certain sense to allow e.g. admin->classroom, even though we haven't really discussed the implications. But other cases are more ambiguous. For instance, what does it mean to have an "admin for a learner group" or a "student for a facility"?

Yeah, we discussed the use cases for having an admin for a classroom (allowing a teacher to manage accounts and groups specifically for their class) and a coach for a facility (allowing a coach to view data for *any* student, without getting write access), but there are a few implications, yeah. I'm not worried about the backend; it will be simple and elegant to check permissions -- given how I've described it in the class-based perms spec doc (having a role for a collection means having that role for all subcollections, and means having that relation to any learners for those subcollections as well). The part I do worry about is the "user management" interfaces for administering these roles, which gets messy if it's not constrained. So that would motivate doing what you're saying, for me (constraining what's "easy" to do in the Python API). But if we are allowing a few different variations of pairings (e.g. admin for both facility and classroom), then it means defining that method multiple times, which isn't super DRY. But the code being repeated is minimal, so perhaps it's worth it. Happy to do that.

> We had discussed having two separate non-abstract user Models -- I think this is a really compelling reason to pursue that. I was worried earlier that it might not play nicely with django.contrib.auth, but based on our previous discussions and the example here I think it's preferable.

Happy to try that again -- it did feel a bit weird when I tried it last time (having `request.user` not always be an instance of `AUTH_USER_MODEL`), but given the reading we did, it shouldn't be a problem in practice, since we're providing our own auth backend. I can try a pass of that in this PR.

Reading around a bit, and given that I have tried all the 'standard' things recommended to improve Webpack builds, and to no avail, I think loaders may be to blame.

It seems that loaders run in series, not parallel, meaning that the more files we add that require loaders (every `.vue` file needs a vue loader, then all the loaders for each component) means that we add to the serial processing time.

Going to try HappyPack to see if it can give a speed boost before I merge this.

The full build is ~22kb and the "core" build is ~4kb. IMO it's useful enough even though there is overlap in ES6 to justify the full build. Regardless the particular case I was thinking of was comparing non-primitive types by value. Non-primitive types are compared by reference in javascript, so that for example:
```js
foo = [1, 2, 3];
bar = [1, 2, 3];
foo == bar; // false
foo === bar; // false again
```

The same holds for objects, Maps, Sets, etc. Lodash has an [isEqual](https://lodash.com/docs#isEqual) method in the core for deep comparison. There's other stuff in the full build that doesn't have a built-in ES6 analog AFAIK (like lots of stuff under the Function heading). So I think including either the core build or the full build would be a good "investment". My 2 cents.

So... no time machine here, but the reason why it ends in 16 instead of 44 is because 444444444444444444 has 59 bits of precision needed to store the number:
   
    >>> from math import log
    >>> log(444444444444444444)/log(2)
    58.62478070653021

Since a python 64-bit double can only store 52 bits of "significance". The extra bits are then rounded off according to IEEE 754 specification.  [More details on wikipedia.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Double-precision_floating-point_format)

django-pyodbc connects through a module called pyodbc. Your dbf needs to have a proper pyodbc connection.  Then, you can tweak the settings, including `left_sql_quote`, and `right_sql_quote` to give it a try.  I go django-pydobc working with IBM DB2, so it could work.  Not sure, however.

import docker
import multiprocessing as mp

def stats(server):
    client=docker.from_env()
    client_lowlevel = docker.APIClient(base_url='unix://var/run/docker.sock')
    client_stats=client_lowlevel.stats(container=server,decode=True, stream=False)

processes =[ mp.Process(target=stats, args=(server)) for server in ['server1','server2']]

# Run processes
for p in processes:
    p.start()

# Exit the completed processes
for p in processes:
    p.join()

It can be done with prevent initialization regex as well (#1405). 

Lets consider two scenarios in which one can transfer weights:

1. model is exactly same but you want to freeze some parameters on continuing training. This is the scenario discussed above. Eg. I trained model with dataA, now want to freeze "linear_x_*" and continue other parameters training on dataB.
2. model itself is new and more complex. I trained modelA on dataA, but now I want to use modelA's trained encoder_x and encoder_y in my new modelB. Moreover, I want to freeze encoder_x and keep encoder_y learnable. This can also be extended for borrowing modules from different trained models.

**Scenario 1** is simpler and should be easily handlable with fine-tune command. Only minor change in fine-tune is required to turn of gradients of relevant parameters. It would be better if regex based support was available in experiment.json to control this. I can send pr for this after discussion here.

**Scenario 2**: With (#1405), there is a support for scenario 2 using `train` command. Below is a bare-minimum sketch of `modelB.py`.

```
def from_params(cls, vocab: Vocabulary, params: Params):
    modelA = load_archive( params.pop("modelA_path")).model
    encoder_x = modelA.encoder_x
    encoder_y = modelA.encoder_y
    initializer_params = params.pop('initializer', [])
    initializer_params += [ [".*encoder_x.*".format(name), "prevent"]  ]  # because it needs to be transferred
    initializer_params += [ [".*encoder_y.*".format(name), "prevent"]  ]  # because it needs to be transferred
    initializer = InitializerApplicator.from_params(initializer_params)

    nograd_module_names = ["encoder_x"]

    return cls(vocab=vocab,
                     encoder_x=encoder_x,
                     encoder_y=encoder_y,
                     nograd_module_names=nograd_module_names)

def __init__(....):

    # do everyting ..

    initializer(self)
    for module_name in nograd_module_names:
        [ p.requires_grad_(False) for p in self._modules[module_name].parameters() ]

```

I have written some utilities to handle more complicated versions of Scenario 2 directly with `experiment.json` so one can do the following:
```
"model":{
        "modelA": {
            "dir": "modelA_ser_dir",
            "borrow_modules": { 
                    "encoder_x" : "transfer" # Only Transfer
                    "encoder_y" : "tune"     # Transfer and Tune
            }
        },
}
```
and have the above effect with 2-3 additional lines using utilities in `from_params`. These utilities were written for my specific purpose because I am borrowing parts and pieces from different models at the same time and need full configurability. But if the usage seems general enough, we can discuss this in separate pr.

The bandpass returns a float image, which is rounded to an integer dtype for performance reasons. (IIRC, I found that the dilation step in particular was much faster for ints.) Before this rounding takes place, the rescaling fills the gamut to retain as much contrast as possible. For uint8, this makes a difference: more detail is kept. But for higher bit depths, I would guess that is makes little if any difference.

Looking back on it now, I think rescaling is too aggressive. The user should do this themselves if it needs to be done at all. Because, of course, if the min and max values of the images vary significantly over time, the scale by which "mass" is measured will be inconsistent over time.

If removing the rescaling altogether does not hurt the subpixel precision to badly (i.e., if the feature tests still pass) then I think it would be fine to remove it for v0.3.

To answer some of your questions:

- ImageJ also puts the origin at the top left; that is the convention for images. If you are seeing agreement that is a good thing.
- a brief description of the columns is at the end of the locate() docstring. Intensity is the same units as the original image. I don’t know exactly how eccentricity is computed for a mass distribution, but if you need to know you can look at the source code or maybe ask @caspervdw .
- if the particle sizes are roughly similar (maybe within a factor of 2) you can probably do a single feature-finding pass. If they are very different you may have to be clever. You could call locate() multiple times and combine the results (and please share your solution!), or you might want to try an altogether different approach, described in the “Bubble tracking in foams” tutorial. Once you have done that, the linking step will work in the same way.
- it is pretty straightforward to select the rows from a DataFrame that match a particle number. However this is probably not the best place to learn Pandas :). There are some excellent tutorials out there (including the official one), not to mention Stack Overflow.

The walkthrough serves as an example for particle tracking, nothing more. Also the filtering is an example. The quality of the video is (in my opinion) not sufficient to accurately measure the diffusion constant of the dispersed particles.

I think it makes no sense to interpret the effect of filtering on the measured diffusivity, as any trend is probably overruled by random errors stemming from the video quality (particle overlap, motion in z-direction, background inhomogeneity, particle size polydispersity, etc.). Considering this, the resulting values you report are all close enough (<10%) to the theoretical value.

Very interesting idea! Gaining particles is not penalized at all, but losing a particle is. The size of this penalty is `search_range`. You can read the Crocker & Grier paper cited in the documentation for details, but my understanding is that the penalty is as large as it needs to be; increasing it will not change the behavior of the algorithm. More concretely, the algorithm evaluates a set of possible links as determined by `search_range`; if the link that you want is not among the possible links (i.e. a distance greater than `search_range`), simply increasing the penalty will not fix the problem.

My advice is to follow @tacaswell 's advice :smile:.

An interesting theoretical question: If the number of particles is truly conserved *and* feature identification is perfect, is Crocker-Grier the best algorithm?

I agree with the assumption that there should be a way to disable dependencies, but this should only happen with long commandline switches and not happen on default. 

I think there is also a third person, which needs to be satisfied: someone who has not a lot of ideas about requirements and such stuff and wants to have certain python packages to "just work". For such persons, having a requirement system which leads a system/env in a broken state, even for same edge cases, is a bad thing.

I think there are a few things which would make this problem a bit easier if these would be addressed:

* Some requirements are not actually requirements for the way you use the packages. The problem here is, that a python package currently can't be broken into multiple conda packages (e.g. matplotlib into several packages with one backend each) -> I don't need a pyqt if I don't use that backend. -> This is https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/793
* There is no automatic checks for (native) dependencies. E.g. debian has a whole battery of tools to make automatic specification of dependencies (at build time) possible. Implementing a default check for all dlls/so files that they don't miss any dependencies during the test phase of the build would make failures like https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/2028 impossible. Same for python packages (see next point).
* Automatic build time specification: it seems possible for debian packages, so why not for conda packages? Debian builds a database for each exported symbol and when it was introduced, so that dependencies can be specified down to the the specific version which implements all symbols which are needed. One could build such a database for all symbols in python as well (e.g. build on https://github.com/bndr/pipreqs). If such a database would exist, then this means that he requirments get less specific and therefore easier to satisfy.

I'm able to reproduce this in 4.0.2. The good news is that I'm not able to reproduce this in master :-) I'm going to try and diagnose it anyway to make sure I understand the cause, but I did some refactoring of version ordering for other reasons that may have caught the error.

The issue isn't quite for a random package, but for a package that is depending on msvc_runtime (which all those packages are on Windows environments).

You may be able to run "conda update msvc_runtime", but that still didn't solve my problems.
Closing as outdated.  Please file a new ticket if you encounter related issues in the future.

@AndreasHeger surely infer_query_length should defer back to the SEQ field in the event of CIGAR field being unavailable?  The [documentation](http://pysam.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#pysam.AlignedSegment.infer_query_length) for `infer_query_length` currently says:

> infer query length from sequence or CIGAR alignment.

which would imply that if the CIGAR was not available then the value of query_length would be supplied.  The documentation for [query_length](http://pysam.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#pysam.AlignedSegment.query_length) says:

> The length of a query is 0 if there is no sequence in the BAM/SAM file. In those cases, the read length can be inferred from the CIGAR alignment, see pysam.AlignedSegment.infer_query_length().

I'm assuming "can" here means it's something the user could do if they got a 0.  If the user wanted the SEQ field length, they could grab that via query_length whereas if the user specifically wanted the query length inferred from the CIGAR.  From the documentation I would have assumed that "infer_query_length" function would try and intuit the original query length based on the evidence available e.g. CIGAR or SEQ len or alternatively be called something like "infer_query_length_from_cigar" (or something less cumbersome!).  Is that something that can be done confidently, or is there an edge case I'm missing here.

If you are in an environment where `GET` with body is not supported please use the `send_get_body_as` parameter and set it to `'POST'` as per (0). That is a mre systematic solution that will work globally and won't rely on every individual call to be annotated with the desired method.

Thank you! So my understanding is that this thing `az resource list --namespace microsoft.insights --resource-type components --query [*].[id,location]` returns the array of arrays. So  `--out tsv` will make every nested array a row and all elements of nested array is a separate column. When this command `az resource show --id "$ID" --query [id,location] --o tsv` returns an array of strings. So `--out tsv` makes every element of it a separate row.

Python 3.3 and PyPy3 are only partially supported because line numbers were removed from `AST.arg`:

```python
# Python 2.7
ipdb> ast.dump(node.args.args[0])
"Name(id='a', ctx=Param())"
ipdb> node.args.args[0].__dict__
{'col_offset': 4, 'id': 'a', 'ctx': <_ast.Param object at 0x7fbebd338190>, 'lineno': 3}

# Python 3.3
ipdb> ast.dump(node.args.args[0])
"arg(arg='a', annotation=None)"
ipdb> node.args.args[0].__dict__
{'annotation': None, 'arg': 'a'}

# Python 3.4
ipdb> ast.dump(node.args.args[0])
"arg(arg='a', annotation=None)"
ipdb> node.args.args[0].__dict__
{'lineno': 3, 'annotation': None, 'col_offset': 4, 'arg': 'a'}
```

It's also noted in [Python 3.3 docs](https://docs.python.org/3.3/library/ast.html):
> arg = (identifier arg, expr? annotation)

Compared to [Python 3.4 docs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/ast.html):
>     arg = (identifier arg, expr? annotation)
           attributes (int lineno, int col_offset)

Technically aiohttp creates a task per client request.
On client disconnection the system stops the task ASAP.
The only way to do it is task cancelling (let's assume web handler is waiting response from DB or other service, we want to cancel it too without waiting for explicit operation over connection to websocket client).

`Task.cancel()` is done by sending `asyncio.CancelledError` exception, the exception class is derived from standard `Exception`. This is asyncio behavior, nothing specific to aiohttp itself.

The only thing I could suggest is catching `CancelledError` in your handler explicitly:
```
try:
    ...
except asyncio.CancelledError:
    pass
except Exception as exc:
    log(exc)
```
Or you could just don't catch so broad type like `Exception`.

For the replace() stuff, it only touches the encoded part of the stream. So wathever the input content is, it can't be converted with "\n" in the base64 format. So we can safely transform it as long as we don't used any of the base64 alphabet.

But since this first implementation has the issue seen earlier, I'll try a smarter approach.

I still don't understand how you don't see this a a critical feature.  The primary use case is you want to guard against a server that has hung (think of FTP use case).  The current impl requires the client to calculate how long the request will take based on your current network conditions, size of file, and load. Furthermore, in the case of a large file, you'll probably sent the timeout to something like 5 minutes, this means it will take you 5 minutes to detect that a server has hung instead of the 2s it takes to receive a chunk and detect the hang. This also means you would have to have different times for different sized files.  Further like I've mentioned before several times network read_timeouts are traditionally handled the way I've mentioned, if you wanted a total timeout it should not have been called read timeout, instead it should have been called request timeout or something else.

Yes, in principle, but it's hard to do. For example, when do we switch over to opt-out? When the next Ubuntu LTS comes out? What makes Ubuntu so special, RHEL and Debian have LTS releases. The reality is that there is no option here that pleases everyone. Someone's code is broken, either explicitly (verification errors) or implicitly (they are operating insecurely and don't know it). The "bundling" approach is only suitable for large teams with enough developer resources to follow all of the transitive dependencies' development to pull in critical bugfixes. The vast majority of developer teams don't have the time nor resources to do this, so the final result is that nobody knows what code is actually being run, how out-of-date this is, and what sort of bugs it contains. This is very easy-to-use from a developer point-of-view, but is a complete abdication of one's responsibility to one's users. Debian and other distros unbundling dependencies, and ensuring that there is only one copy of any particular piece of software available in the whole distribution, helps to avoid this mess and is a very efficient way of using volunteer resources to provide these QA guarantees.

Every new version of a given package that is kept coinstallable with other versions (e.g. because they are bundled as dependencies of two different larger pieces of software), is effectively keeping a fork of that package, along with all the maintenance costs it brings. Ignoring these costs and pretending they don't exist, just piles up the technical debt and means users end up running unmaintained software.

I think for server extensions it makes sense to use entry points. It's less clear for nbextensions: they are usually installed and served through Python machinery, but in principle there's nothing Python specific about them, and we may one day regret tying them to Python mechanisms. On the other hand, if server extensions are loaded by entry points, it would be a nice symmetry for nbextensions to use the same, and it would mean that extensions could be cleanly uninstalled with the same tools used to install them.


This also ties into our discussion on #2824 about moving to a model where installed extensions are automatically loaded unless they have been disabled, rather than requiring an enable step as we currently do.

See also my [entrypoints module](http://entrypoints.readthedocs.io/en/latest/), which we're already using in nbconvert.

@ericmjl instead of defining the gradient for `np.take` I used 'fancy indexing' to get the same result (which `autograd` already knows how to differentiate).

Say I want to take the `index` set from an array `A` using `np.take`, giving `A_index`

`
A_index = np.take(A,index)
`

You can also do this using fancy indexing as follows (seems just as fast)

`
A_index = A.flatten()[index]
`

and `autograd` can compute gradients on this version already.

I'm pretty sure the fix is going to be checking the length of `ls_tree.split()`, just like you mentioned, and then skipping that submodule. What I'd like to figure out if I can, though, is how the https://github.com/accelio/accelio repo got into the state it's in.

Normally when you add a submodule, git puts two things in your repo: an entry in the `.gitmodules` file, and also a submodule object at the place in the tree where your submodule will go. (In an actual checkout this submodule object becomes an empty directory before initialization, and then a full clone after.) You can see this in the [YouCompleteMe repo](https://github.com/Valloric/YouCompleteMe), where the [paths in the `.gitmodules` file](https://github.com/Valloric/YouCompleteMe/blob/master/.gitmodules#L2) correspond to these [visible submodule objects](https://github.com/Valloric/YouCompleteMe/tree/master/third_party) in the tree.

Accelio seems to have gotten into a state where it [has a `.gitmodules` entry](https://github.com/accelio/accelio/blob/master/.gitmodules#L2) but [no corresponding submodule objects in the tree](https://github.com/accelio/accelio/tree/master/src/kernel/compat). Do you know how that might've happened? Maybe someone did something weird by hand? It's simple enough to detect this case and ignore it, but I want to make sure that's the right thing to do.

Update: Ok, I think I figured out how they ended up that way: https://github.com/accelio/accelio/issues/113. I'll go ahead with the fix.

You're not wrong. The original intent for `pie` was to make a simple, obvious interface for KMIP operations. The original `KMIPProxy` requires you to prebuild your attribute objects manually, which is messy and not well documented. It also exposes way too much of the internal library guts, which can change. The plan was always to add on arguments to the `ProxyKmipClient` methods as needed, so adding something like `cryptographic_usage_mask` is fine.

I've toyed with just adding a `**kwargs` field to each client method and calling it a day, but I prefer having explicit arguments. They're self documenting and it just makes things clearer for developers. Of course, with something like `create_key_pair` this could get really messy, since you theoretically could have tons of unique attributes for both public and private keys.

What/how many specific arguments do you need and for which operations?

@further-reading : Per the API spec the `max` parameter specifies the [maximum number of items per page](https://developer.ciscospark.com/pagination.html#controlling-page-size) ("page size").

Since ciscosparkapi transparently handles paging for you… It respectfully uses your requested page size, and still returns the all of the results - requesting additional pages as needed until all results have been returned. 😁

If you only want a certain number of items returned, you can use the Python standard library's [`itertools.islice()`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/itertools.html#itertools.islice) function to get just what you need:

``` python
from itertools import islice

from ciscosparkapi import CiscoSparkAPI

api = CiscoSparkAPI()

messages = api.messages.list(roomId='<your-room-id>')

# Only want the first 50 messages?
for message in islice(messages, 50):
    print(message)

# Want them in a list?
short_list = list(islice(messages, 50))
```

I have considered adding a convenience slicing ability to the package's GeneratorContainers, which would effectively do the very same thing only with the simple syntax of `messages[:50]`.

I have noted the extra initialization temp_buf_size and will add that fix to our next release.

I2C addresses are 7 bits. The 508 uses the upper most 7 bits of the address byte to identify that address while Linux uses the lower 7 bits. The address 0x60 as identified by linux when shifted left is 0xC0 which is the default address of the 508.

> @mwpowellhtx let's assume WinForms/WPF can incorporate Core/Standard bits. Fair enough; targeting what runtime(s)? Framework 4.6.2, for instance?

You will in future be able to use WPF targeting .Net Core as a runtime instead of .Net Framework. You can already use .Net Standard libraries. Both frameworks support .Net Standard 2.0.

> you are also assuming calls for a WinForms/WPF port into "native" Standard/Core, so to speak, is to the exclusion of other delivery modes... GUIs are indeed dominant across most platforms in the desktop world.

.Net core does/will run across mobile devices and desktops, which have UIs, and servers, most of which don't. It would be similarly crazy to put "make a phone call" inside .Net core as would be to put "display a ribbon" in there.

> some of us might like to run a GUI cross-plat, but are not willing to buy into the Xamarin cost of doing business.

Of course I would be happy to see an approach with lower costs. I assume this refers to performance or reliability? Unfortunately the community commentary about what Microsoft should do instead has not attempted to tackle the serious problems posed by the current multitude of operating systems and platform types.

> With a weak promise of WinForms/WPF comprehension coming in, let's say, v3.x, to be accurate. Until it happens, this will continue to be the criticism, at least for my part.

The ability of WPF projects to use .Net Core has very little relationship to cross platform development. It's a convenience for WPF developers, who can take advantage of developments and performance improvements in .Net Core, and for Microsoft which can stop developing .Net Framework and focus on .Net Core.

A workaround for the people affected by this: 

As the stacktrace suggests, something goes wrong in creating a new virtualhost. If you set up an SSL enabled virtualhost for the domain yourself (without any certificates in it) and run letsencrypt-auto again, it will work. For ubuntu/Debian create the file  `/etc/apache2/sites-available/example.com.conf`. No need to enable, letsencrypt will do that for you.

Example vhost file (check your ciphers/protocols):

```
<VirtualHost *:443>
        ServerAdmin info@example.com
        ServerName example.com
		ServerAlias www.example.com

        DocumentRoot /var/www/example.com/public_html/
		
        ErrorLog /path/to/logs/error.log

        # Possible values include: debug, info, notice, warn, error, crit,
        # alert, emerg.
        LogLevel warn

        CustomLog /path/to/logs/ssl_access.log combined

        #   SSL Engine Switch:
        #   Enable/Disable SSL for this virtual host.
        SSLEngine on
        SSLProtocol all -SSLv2 -SSLv3
        SSLHonorCipherOrder on
        SSLCipherSuite ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA384:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA128:DHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA384:DHE-RSA-AES128-GCM-SHA128:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA384:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA128:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA:ECDHE-RSA-AES128-SHA:DHE-RSA-AES128-SHA128:DHE-RSA-AES128-SHA128:DHE-RSA-AES128-SHA:DHE-RSA-AES128-SHA:ECDHE-RSA-DES-CBC3-SHA:EDH-RSA-DES-CBC3-SHA:AES128-GCM-SHA384:AES128-GCM-SHA128:AES128-SHA128:AES128-SHA128:AES128-SHA:AES128-SHA:DES-CBC3-SHA:HIGH:!aNULL:!eNULL:!EXPORT:!DES:!MD5:!PSK:!RC4
		
		# Certificates
        SSLCertificateFile    /this/will/be/replaced/by/letsencrypt
        SSLCertificateKeyFile /this/will/be/replaced/by/letsencrypt/too

        <FilesMatch "\.(cgi|shtml|phtml|php)$">
                SSLOptions +StdEnvVars
        </FilesMatch>
        <Directory /usr/lib/cgi-bin>
                SSLOptions +StdEnvVars
        </Directory>
        BrowserMatch "MSIE [2-6]" \
                nokeepalive ssl-unclean-shutdown \
                downgrade-1.0 force-response-1.0
        # MSIE 7 and newer should be able to use keepalive
        BrowserMatch "MSIE [17-9]" ssl-unclean-shutdown

</VirtualHost>
```

This is an unambigous win over the current code.

To see what the best thing to do is, I made two versions, `parserinfo` does the `min` call in the constructor and stores it as a private attribute, and `parserinfo2` is the code as presented here.  and `parserinfo3` is the code as is now:

```python
>>> i = parserinfo()
>>> i2 = parserinfo2()
>>> i3 = parserinfo3()

>>> %timeit i.weekday('Wed')
1000000 loops, best of 3: 627 ns per loop

>>> %timeit i2.weekday('Wed')
1000000 loops, best of 3: 393 ns per loop

>>> %timeit i3.weekday('Wed')
100000 loops, best of 3: 3.4 µs per loop

>>> %timeit i.weekday('A')
1000000 loops, best of 3: 285 ns per loop

>>> %timeit i2.weekday('A')
1000000 loops, best of 3: 724 ns per loop

>>> %timeit i3.weekday('A')
100000 loops, best of 3: 2.83 µs per loop
```

When the string passed to `weekday` has length >= the shortest string in in the `weekday` keys, this version is best. When it's `<=` the min, the cached version is best. The version we have now performs worst in all cases.

I'm not sure all the code paths for the common case, but I'm guessing that it's actually kinda rare to hit the case where `weekday` or `month` are passed 2-character strings, so this approach seems the best one to me.

@sondree @bwbeach I've looked at how `B2Api` handles it. It is `delete_file_version(self, file_id, file_name)`. I am going to make a PR with a simple pass-through between `Bucket` and `B2Api` in this regard, lets move the discussion there.

Here is some background information on how uploading and file integrity checking works in B2 CLI. We have to differentiate between "large" (per default larger than 100MB) and "small" files.

Small files are uploaded in a single transaction. During the upload the SHA1 of the file is calculated and sent after the actual content of the file. The B2 server also calculates the SHA1 hash and verifies it against the hash sent by the client. The server’s response contains this hash which is verified again on the client side. This is the checksum your PR will display.

Large files are uploaded as smaller parts in multiple transactions. For each part the SHA1 is verified just as for small files. However, there is no SHA1 checksum calculated for the whole file by default. You can specify the whole SHA1 hash yourself using the `--sha1` option, but this is not calculated or verified by the server. So it can't be used to verify file integrity because it's always the hash you provided.

Regarding your PR, I think it could be useful to print the contentSha1 when it's available (which is only the case for small files). Anyway, verifying the file integrity using this hash is redundant, because this is already done by B2 CLI.

Yes, it is a limitation of current design: on schema instantiation all field objects are bound to that particular instance. Through this binding they obtain context. If you will create multiple schemas, all field objects will bind to the last one created.

Solution: do not create multiple schemas, reuse a single instance.

Playing around with the allocators was mainly my own curiosity (which killed the proverbial cat, I hear), not so much a real suggestion.

Building upon `std::vector` is also totally possible, but will effectively cost 8 bytes, as `sizeof(vector<T>)` turns out to be 24 on x86_64. The std::vector allocator template parameter even allows us to retain the tracking mechanism currently in place. I am however not sure how aggressively `std::vector` utilizes `realloc` and friends (I tuned it quite aggressively, as you can see by the silenced warning ;) ).

Of course std::vector also does not provide the small string optimization of buffer, but that mainly relates to creation not access. (In fact I can even imagine it having weird effects on access due to reducing cache-locality on the heap.) Interestingly, as you noted that memory allocation is limited during search, it may even turn out that buffer is actually counter-productive, as it incurs a slight access penalty...

Size tracking on the heap (as is done in the current Z3 vector) effectively saves 8 bytes (or 50%) on *unused* (not just empty, but capacity == 0) vectors while seemingly slightly reducing performance. It is my guess that the trade-off is not worth it, but I have not really measured impact on memory usage so far.

> I could also use 2D convolutions without having to convert the image to greyscale before I feed it to Lasagne. But the results with 3D convolutions might be better than with 2D convolutions. Correct?

No. If you have a three-channel image of shape `(3, h, w)`, there are three options for a 3D convolutional layer:
* kernel shape `(3, kh, kw)`: this convolves the three channels with three separate kernels and adds up the results, which is exactly the same as using a 2D convolution
* kernel shape `(1, kh, kw)`: this convolves each channel separately with the same kernel and leaves the results separated, which is exactly the same as reshaping the input to three times the batchsize and a single input channel, applying a 2D convolution and reshaping back
* kernel shape `(2, kh, kw)`: this convolves and merges the red and green channel, then convolves and merges the green and blue channel using the same kernels as for the first operation, which doesn't make sense

Hah, wait a second, the first dimension should just be set to `None` for the recurrent connection layers, and then it works fine.  I changed the test to do that and it should pass on Travis.  This is an interesting failure mode though.

On the other hand, `NonlinearityLayer` sounds as if it was a specialty of this layer to sport a nonlinearity. I think it's okay to regard `NonlinearityOnlyLayer` as a stripped-down version of usual layers that do something *besides* applying a nonlinearity.

By the way, if `nonlinearity` would default to `None` instead of the linear rectifier, we could also just add that keyword to every layer for convenience... :)

21.0%    84.4%       1.100s       1.10e-03s   1000     4   GpuDot22(GpuFromHost.0, W)
 9.3%    93.7%       0.487s       4.87e-04s   1000     5   GpuGemm{inplace}(GpuDot22.0, TensorConstant{1.0}, GpuFromHost.0, W, TensorConstant{1.0})

And for concatenation:

21.0%    75.3%       1.285s       1.29e-03s   1000     4   GpuDot22(GpuJoin.0, W)

@mrsmith203 

Did you yet download and install the latest pyspectral?

There you have a template config file where this is attempted explained.
It starts:

```
[general]
# Here you put the path to the internal hdf5 formatet rsr data
# These data can be downloaded from the internet, see pyspectral documentation
# at https://pyspectral.readthedocs.org/
# If you have these hdf5 files available you will not need to read the original
# rsr data specified in the path's below, and you can leave "path" empty.
rsr_dir = /path/to/internal/rsr_data

[Meteosat-9-seviri]
path = /path/to/original/seviri/rsr/data/MSG_SEVIRI_Spectral_Response_Characterisation.XLS
# You can provide any file name as you wish but it has to end with ".npz":
tb2rad_lut_filename = /path/to/radiance/tb/lut/data/tb2rad_lut_meteosat9_seviri_ir3.9.npz
```

So, under [general] you put the path to your recently downloaded hdf5 files with relative spectral response data.

And under each satellite section, e.g. [EOS-Aqua-modis] for Aqua MODIS, you put a valid path (including filename) to an npz file (numpy data dump file format) where you will store radiance to tb conversions once you have run pyspctral once! This file does not exist until you have specified it in the config file and run pyspectral. It is a cache file, that allows to save memory and processing time.

In my case I have:

tb2rad_lut_filename = /tmp/tb2rad_lut_aqua_modis_ir3.7.npz

But the value of `tb2rad_lut_filename` can be anything, except the directory needs to exist.

How about this:

* There are three options, `--maxRows`, `--sampleMethod`, and `--sampleFraction` (abbreviated as `-n`, `-m`, `-f`)

* `--sampleMethod` can be either `"take"` or `"sample"`

* `--maxRows` can either be a positive integer N or some sentinel value (like `None`).

* `--sampleFraction` is equal to some float F where 0 <= F <= 1

* When `--sampleMethod == "take"` and `--maxRows=N`, that's equivalent to calling `sql(...).take(N)`

* When `--sampleMethod == "sample"` and `--maxRows=N` and `--sampleFraction=F`, that's equivalent to calling `sql(...).sample(False, F).take(N)`

* When `--sampleMethod == "take"` and `--maxRows=None`, that's equivalent to calling `sql(...).collect()`

* When `--sampleMethod == "sample"` and `--maxRows=N` and `--sampleFraction=F`, that's equivalent to calling `sql(...).sample(False, F).collect()`

We would also add 3 configurations for the default values of these options.

Are you using the D435 at 848x480 resolution? This may be an artifact of the low resolution at a far distance.  No post-processing is done. Note that the D435 has a 90HFOV and 50mm baseline, vs the R200 56 HFOV and 70mm baseline. If you run them at the same resolution, then you will get worse results with the D435. 

The D435 is meant for wide FOV near range usages. Have you considered using the D415 instead? It has higher depth resolution (55mm baseline, 65HFOV, and uses 2MP imagers with depth output at 1280x720).
 
Finally, is this a single point depth data, or is it done by averaging the depth in certain areas?

For posterity, here's the procedure for getting, inspecting and merging a pull request. First, edit `.git/config`. Find the `[origin]` section (or whatever section points at this repository - for me it's `[upstream]`) and add the following **before** the existing fetch line:

    fetch = +refs/pull/*/head:refs/remotes/origin/pr/*

Then do a `git fetch`. You'll end up with this!

![screenshot_2016-02-12_18-16-34](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1012989/13023233/fe6edae0-d1b4-11e5-8e5b-03f342bc8cfd.png)

Neat, right? Here's a typical workflow for inspecting, testing and merging a pull request:

```sh
git checkout upstream/pr/106
# inspect changes and test them out
git rebase master
# test them out again to ensure nothing broke
git branch -f master && git checkout master
git push origin master && git push upstream master
```

There's plenty of other similar workflows. See https://github.com/kahmali/meteor-restivus/issues/89#issuecomment-117805045 for another testimonial.

Well, so in my opinion, the issue is with _both_ Pipenv _and_ this library:

* Pipenv should not be reporting "module not found" when it gets an `ImproperlyConfigured` error when trying to import a module, it should just propagate the exception.

* The root module of third-party Django packages like this one should not be throwing an `ImproperlyConfigured` error when being imported, because it prevents development tools like Pipenv from discovering metadata about them, such as where they're located.  As a result, right now it's not possible for me to open this project's source code in my local editor via `pipenv open` unless I set `DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE` in my terminal environment, which is not (IMO) something Django developers should be expected to do.

A little more information: If we do away with the `DjangoFilterConnectionField`s and define our connections using only `DjangoConnectionField`s, the lambda trick works.

```python
from django.db import models
from graphene import ObjectType, Schema, relay
from graphene_django import DjangoObjectType, DjangoConnectionField


class Recipe(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=50)
    ingredients = models.ManyToManyField('Ingredient', related_name='recipes')


class Ingredient(models.Model):
    name = models.CharField(max_length=50)


class RecipeNode(DjangoObjectType):

    ingredients = DjangoConnectionField(lambda: IngredientNode)

    class Meta:
        interfaces = [relay.Node]
        model = Recipe
        only_fields = ['name']


class IngredientNode(DjangoObjectType):

    recipes = DjangoConnectionField(RecipeNode)

    class Meta:
        interfaces = [relay.Node]
        model = Ingredient
        only_fields = ['name']


class Queries(ObjectType):

    all_recipes = DjangoConnectionField(RecipeNode)
    all_ingredients = DjangoConnectionField(IngredientNode)


schema = Schema(query=Queries)
```

@kvey you don't need to reimplement `convert_column_to_string`. You can import it from `graphene_sqlalchemy.converter` and register your `S3FileReferenceType` type in a single line.

```
from sqlalchemy import types
from sqlalchemy.types import TypeDecorator
from graphene import String
from graphene_sqlalchemy.converter import convert_sqlalchemy_type, convert_column_to_string

class S3FileReferenceType(TypeDecorator):
    impl = types.Text
## --- rest of class

convert_sqlalchemy_type.register(S3FileReferenceType)(convert_column_to_string)

Right, okay. So TensorBoard is working properly, but you have bad data. It looks like you wrote multiple TensorBoard runs into the same directory, with something like
```
$ cat my_script.py
import tensorflow as tf
writer = tf.summary.FileWriter("./output")
summary = tf.summary.scalar(321.0)
with tf.Session() as sess:
  writer.add_graph(sess.graph)
  writer.add_summary(sess.run(summary))
writer.close()
$ python my_script.py
$ python my_script.py  # now you have conflicting data!!
```
TensorBoard is behaving as expected. The easiest way to resolve this is to ensure that you are writing to distinct directories with each `FileWriter`. If you're careful, you can ensure the looser condition that across all `FileWriter`s for a single directory you only call `add_graph` once, and each time you call `add_summary` you pass a globally unique value for `global_step`.

Yeah, larger packets tend to have some trouble with 6LoWPAN fragmentation. If your office is anything like the offices I have worked in, 2.4GHz is going to have a lot of "noise" on it. The more 6LoWPAN fragmentation that occurs, the chances of the entire packet being dropped (because of a dropped 6LoWPAN fragment) increase pretty dramatically.

This is one of the big reasons why the application-level protocols intended for these devices need to keep their packet sizes as small as possible.

Campaign Monitor's transactional sending looks like a good match for Anymail.

If anyone else would like to see this, please vote for it with GitHub's [thumbs-up reaction](https://blog.github.com/2016-03-10-add-reactions-to-pull-requests-issues-and-comments/). And add a comment if you'd need to use OAuth rather than API keys...

Campaign Monitor offers [two kinds](https://www.campaignmonitor.com/api/getting-started/#authentication) of API auth: API keys would be straightforward to use with Anymail. OAuth would be more complicated (because Anymail doesn't have any way to store and update access tokens). If anyone wanting Campaign Monitor support in Anymail has a need for OAuth, it would be really helpful to understand that use case—please add a comment.

@protactinium if you'd like to take a shot at an integration, the [adding ESPs](https://github.com/anymail/django-anymail/blob/master/ADDING_ESPS.md) doc is where to start. I'm happy to answer questions or help on a work-in-progress pull request.

It looks like Campaign Monitor's "[smart email](https://www.campaignmonitor.com/api/transactional/#send-smart-email)" maps to Anymail's template/merge_data send, and their "[classic email](https://www.campaignmonitor.com/api/transactional/#send-classic-email)" would be used for normal sends.

Campaign Monitor's [webhooks](https://www.campaignmonitor.com/api/webhooks/#currently-available-webhooks) don't seem to include status tracking for sent messages, and they don't offer inbound handling, so the Anymail integration would just be an EmailBackend for sending.

Their Python package [seems to be a thin wrapper](https://github.com/campaignmonitor/createsend-python/blob/v5.0.0/lib/createsend/transactional.py#L31-L66) over the HTTP API calls, and I'm not sure it adds much value. It might be easier to just use Anymail's RequestsBackend to post directly to their API endpoint. (Their wrapper helps a little with OAuth, if there's a need for that, but it doesn't provide a complete solution.)

It is true that certbot is an effort to increase everyone's security and that we do generally want people to improve their security practices globally.
That said, I fear that if we're hostile to people who lack deep knowledge of the world of security software they will become discouraged and not even take the first step to improving their security. This is one of the reasons why the way you express this knowledge to people who are asking for advice really matters.
I don't think it's our place to tell people which software to use or not to use - as many people have different considerations for making those decisions and almost uniformly won't give us enough context to judge that decision. Instead I think it's best to provide constructive feedback to help them use Certbot, and share the knowledge we have that can help them in that effort.
In this example I think it would have been more instructive to say that certbot 0.8.1 is outdated, give advice on how to update the system packages on arch, and then recommend that @lautiamkok try again with a newer version of certbot. But I don't see how leading with a dismissal of the user's whole system is productive.
That all said, I do appreciate that you're providing instructive context for people who are having problems! I just really want to try hard not to discourage any users who are attempting to practice better security.

> Does `-m venv` not work when running under a virtualenv? 

It does not. Creating a python3 venv while a virtualenv is active results in a broken venv. Any other combination will work. Notably, this affects Travis, where the environment is initialized with an activated virtualenv. 

`real_python3()` is checking to see if we're working under an active virtualenv, which we can detect as virtualenv sets `sys.base_prefix`. If we're operating under an active venv, python3 sets `sys.real_prefix`. I should probably move the `isfile()` check into a separate assertion, as this currently assumes that a valid `sys.base_prefix` will imply the existence of `./bin/python3`. 

> The docstring for that function should probably explain more about why it's doing what it's doing rather than what.

Good point.

During the optimization process, TPOT passes whatever is in the `cv` parameter directly to the `cross_val_score` `cv` parameter. Thus, the 'training' score is from whatever cross-validation scheme you passed to the `cv` parameter.

You're correct that the generated Python code from the `export` function is based on a fixed template that uses `train_test_split` for the initial train/test split on the data. We don't have the `export` function generate code with a cross-validation scheme, so there is no configuration for that. You could add a cross-validation scheme yourself by, e.g.,

```Python
training_features, testing_features, training_target, testing_target = \
train_test_split(features, tpot_data['target'].values, random_state=42)

# Score on the training set was:0.7281553398058253
exported_pipeline = make_pipeline(
    FeatureAgglomeration(affinity="l1", linkage="average"),
    DecisionTreeClassifier(criterion="gini", max_depth=10, min_samples_leaf=5, min_samples_split=11)
)

cross_val_score(exported_pipeline, training_features, training_target, cv=LeaveOneOut())
```

Essentially, the generated Python code is meant only to be a starting point for you to integrate the pipeline into your own ML workflow. The `exported_pipeline` (and related imports) are the important part.

I've tried the same but no luck, still get the same error.

There are potentially two different issues here. The first one is that the implementation of `Check` by the provider registry returns an error when `Configure` or `CheckConfig` fails instead of populating the `failures` field of `CheckResponse`. This leads us to drop those errors (see also https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/issues/1712, since it is basically impossible today to reason about who logs what error).

I'm not sure what's going on with Joe's original Azure example. Looks like it's probably a similar issue.

Either way, a band-aid to make this much better until we tackle #1712 in M17 shouldn't be too bad here.

> Just curious, will this work even if you are missing a tsconfig.json file? Might be another nice thing to support to cut down on boilerplate (we would need to seed the various "defaults" we assume, like ES6 modules).

Indeed - would love to know the answer.  From when I looked at this last, I believe it was possible to use with `tsconfig.json`, but that not having this file lead to trouble using tools like VSCode.  Would be good to validate both sides of that as they may have changed.

Generally speaking the format is the same as the original stream, and the same data goes to VLC as goes to a file. The container format of the stream depends on the provider and the stream type. HLS (which is what twitch uses) is TS (transport stream, could also be mp4 but that isn't supported in streamlink), rtmp and HDS are flv, HLS multi audio is repacked in to an mkv. Always the video and audio code remain unchanged (except in specific cases and the use of the transcode options).

You can define your own fields and add new templates. The process is roughly:

1. Find the template folder in this repo or locally.
2. Choose a similar template as base and look into the [template docs](https://github.com/invoice-x/invoice2data/blob/master/TUTORIAL.rst).
3. Make your own new template based on debugging and looking at OCR output.
4. Add your own template folder to the templates to be loaded.
5. Done.
